nativeRange.habitat: Permanent ponds, springs, reservoirs, and seasonal streams in the endorheic drainage of the Etzatlán region, Jalisco; turbid and shallow water no more than 1.5 m deep with muddy or gravel substrates; also clear-water habitats with aquatic vegetation

decorPreferences: dense planting (Java fern, hornwort, water sprite, floating plants for fry refuge)
good water current and surface agitation
dark substrate to enhance male coloration
rocks or driftwood to break line of sight

diet.notes: Accepts most standard aquarium foods readily. Plant matter should form a significant portion of the diet — offer spirulina flakes, vegetable flakes, blanched greens (lettuce, dandelion, chickweed), and algae wafers. Also accepts high-quality flake, micro-pellets, and small frozen/live foods (daphnia, brine shrimp nauplii). Diets excessively high in animal protein may cause digestive issues; maintain a balanced omnivore menu with a herbivore lean.

goodWith: robust small livebearers in species-community setups
larger fast-moving peaceful fish that can tolerate fin nipping
avoidWith: small shrimp (predation risk on fry and juveniles)

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species page image for Xenotoca doadrioi; check page for photographer credit and license before use.

summary: The San Marcos Redtail Splitfin is a critically endangered Mexican Goodeid split from X. eiseni in 2016, endemic to highland springs near Etzatlán, Jalisco. Males display vivid orange-red caudal coloration and blue flanks. Cool-tolerant and viviparous with placenta-like trophotaeniae; popular in conservation breeding programs.
careNotes:
Xenotoca doadroi was formally described as its own species by Domínguez-Domínguez, Bernal-Zuñiga & Piller in 2016, split from the broadly distributed X. eiseni. It is endemic to the endorheic drainage surrounding Arroyo San Marcos and nearby springs in the Etzatlán region of Jalisco, Mexico, and is listed as Critically Endangered by the IUCN (2019), with over half of known wild populations already lost to habitat destruction, water extraction, and introduced species. All hobby specimens are captive-bred, and the species is a flagship for conservation-oriented livebearer keeping.

Like all members of family Goodeidae, X. doadroi is viviparous with a true internal gestation; developing embryos attach to trophotaeniae — ribbon-like placenta-like structures that transfer nutrients from mother to fry. This means females do not store sperm and must be remated for each brood, unlike poeciliid livebearers. Gestation lasts approximately 7–8 weeks, and females typically produce 10–50 large fry (approximately 15 mm) every 60 days or so.

This species is cool-tolerant and does not require a heater in most temperate homes; sustained temperatures above 25°C should be avoided. Aquarium Glaser and experienced keepers recommend providing a seasonal cool-down (17–20°C in winter) to mimic natural conditions and promote long-term health and breeding readiness. The tank should have moderate-to-good water current (powerhead or canister outlet), and generous water changes of 50–70% weekly are widely advised, as goodeids in general are sensitive to accumulated waste. A 60 cm minimum footprint tank suits a group of six; larger groups benefit from more space to reduce male chase behavior.

Males exhibit vivid coloration — orange-red on the caudal peduncle and tail, blue iridescence on the anterior flanks, and a golden ‘saddle’ marking — most intense in dominant, well-conditioned fish. Females are more cryptically brown with dark blotches. Males chase each other and harass females persistently; a 2:1 female-to-male ratio or higher is recommended. The species has a documented fin-nipping reputation; avoid housing with long-finned or slow-moving tankmates. Corydoras catfish have been noted as particular targets of aggression.

Newborn fry are large enough to accept crushed flake and micro-pellets immediately, and are typically left alone by adults after the first few hours, but providing floating plants or a breeder net for the first drops improves survival. A diet leaning toward plant matter (spirulina, vegetable flakes, blanched greens) is essential for long-term health; diets too rich in animal protein are associated with digestive problems in this species and other goodeids.
breedingNotes: Xenotoca doadroi is easy to breed once a healthy group is established. Unlike poeciliid livebearers, females cannot store sperm and require a new mating for each brood. Gestation lasts approximately 7–8 weeks (60 days), producing 10–50 fully formed fry per drop. Fry are large at birth (approximately 15 mm) and free-swimming within hours; they accept finely crushed flake and micro-pellets immediately and do not require infusoria or specialty fry foods. Floating plants such as water sprite or hornwort provide refuge for newborns during the vulnerable first hours. Adults generally ignore fry once they become active swimmers, but separating the first drop into a breeder net or fry tank substantially improves yields. Subsequent broods are less prone to predation once juveniles are present in the colony. Maintain a skewed sex ratio (2–3 females per male) to reduce female harassment. A cool-season period (reducing temperature to 17–20°C for 6–8 weeks) can stimulate renewed breeding activity in established groups.

primary.name: FishBase — Xenotoca doadrioi
primary.notes: Primary taxonomic and biological reference. Confirmed: adult size males 3.7 cm SL, females 4.7 cm SL; order Cyprinodontiformes, family Goodeidae; endemic to endorheic Etzatlán region, Jalisco, Mexico; habitat includes turbid shallow ponds, springs, reservoirs, and seasonal streams up to 1.5 m deep; IUCN CR status (2017 assessment).

additional.name: Aquarium Glaser — Xenotoca doadrioi
additional.notes: Trade and husbandry source. Confirmed: temperature 17–25°C, seasonal winter cool-down 17–20°C recommended, permanent temps above 25°C harmful; pH ~7.5–8, medium hardness; large water changes 60–70% beneficial; green food should form a large dietary component; trophotaeniae noted for goodeid embryonic nutrition; all hobby stock captive-bred.

additional.name: Chicago Area Livebearer Society — Xenotoca doadrioi profile
additional.notes: Keeper experience profile. Confirmed: temperature 60–75°F (15.5–24°C), minimum group of 6 in 10–20-gallon long (38–76 L); males 1.75" (4.4 cm), females 2.0" (5.1 cm); gestation ~2 months, 5–20 fry per drop; fry ~15 mm at birth; fin-nipping reputation noted; bristlenose plecos cited as compatible; species-only or conservation colony approach recommended given rarity.

additional.name: SeriouslyFish — Xenotoca eiseni (closest described relative)
additional.notes: Reference species (X. eiseni, close congener from which X. doadroi was split 2016). Cross-checked: water parameters pH 6.0–8.0, hardness 10–20 dH, temperature 15–30°C; minimum tank 55 L; females do not store sperm; gestation 7–8 weeks, 5–50 large fry; unpredictable aggression toward Corydoras noted; dark substrate for best coloration; diet undemanding but accepts most foods. Parameters and behavior data considered applicable to X. doadroi given recent conspecific status.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Xenotoca doadrioi
additional.notes: Taxonomic summary. Confirmed: formally described 2016 by Domínguez-Domínguez, Bernal-Zuñiga & Piller, split from X. eiseni alongside X. lyonsi; IUCN Critically Endangered (2019); endemic to Etzatlán area, Jalisco; males orange-red caudal coloration, blue-anterior flanks, golden saddle mark; females brownish with dark blotches.

fish.breedingNotes: Viviparous goodeid; gestation ~7–8 weeks producing 10–50 large fry (~15 mm) per brood every ~60 days. Females cannot store sperm and must be remated for each brood. Fry accept crushed flake immediately; floating plants improve early survival. Cool seasonal period (17–20°C) can stimulate spawning cycles.
